coredata.dc:title: Ag0 decorated Cr2S3 NPs embedded on PVP matrix: A colorimetric probe for selective and rapid detection of sulphide ions from environmental samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kokilavani, S.
Syed, Asad
Thomas, Ajith M.
Elgorban, Abdallah M.
Bahkali, Ali H.
Zaghloul, Nouf S.S.
Raju, Lija L.
Sudheer Khan, S.
coredata.dc:description: Globally, the environmental pollution is one of the major issues causing toxicity towards human and aquatic life. We have developed a facile and innovative sensing approach for detection of sulphide ions (S2−) present in the aqueous media using Ag0 decorated Cr2S3 NPs embedded on PVP matrix (Ag/Cr2S3-PVP). Based on the SPR phenomena, the detection of S2− ions was established. The nanohybrid was characterized using various techniques such as UV–vis spectrophotometer, High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Thermal Gravimetric Analysis (TGA), X-ray diffraction analysis(XRD), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The yellowish colour of Ag/Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid turned to brown colour in presence of S2− ions. The selectivity and sensitivity of the prepared probe was studied against the other interfering metal ions. In addition, the effect of different concentration of S2− ions in the nanohybrid solution was investigated and the Limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 6.6 nM. The good linearity was found over the range of 10 nM to 100 μM with R2 value of 0.981. The paper strip based probe was developed for rapid onsite monitoring of S2− ions. The proposed method is found to be cost-effective, rapid, and simple. We have validated the practical applicability of the prepared probe for determining the concentration of S2− ions in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag/Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid
Sulphide ions
Detection
Nanomolar

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the analytical process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors sincerely thank the management of Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Tamil Nadu for providing the necessary facilities for carrying out this work. The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP-2021/56), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–visible absorption spectra of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid (b) XRD spectra of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP, Cr2S3, (c) FTIR spectrum of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP, Cr2S3 and PVP-Ag2S@Cr2S3.
(a) TEM image of (a) Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid, (b)TEM image with inset SAED pattern and (c) TEM image with d-spacing values of Ag and Cr2S3 .
XPS spectra of the Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid (a) survey spectrum (b) S2p (C) O 1s (d) Cr 2p (e) Ag 3d.

(a) Image of different amino acids interacted with Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of different metal ions with nanohybrid and (c) different concentration of S2− with inset calibration curve and (d) interference studies on the detection of S2− ions.
Optimization studies (a) pH, (b) salinity and (c) temperature.
Paper strip based probe for the selective detection of S2− ions (a) Interacted with different metal ions (b) Different concentration of S2− ions.
Possible mechanism for the detection of S2− ions by the prepared probe.
Comparison of detection of sulphide ions by the prepared probes with reported literature.

row.entry: N and S co-doped rose CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Real sample analysis for the detection of S2− ions.

head.ce:title: Ag0 decorated Cr2S3 NPs embedded on PVP matrix: A colorimetric probe for selective and rapid detection of sulphide ions from environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Botany and Biotechnology, St Xavier's College, Thumba, Thiruvananthapuram, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bristol Centre for Functional Nanomaterials, HH Wills Physics Laboratory, Tyndall Avenue, University of Bristol, Bristol BS8 1FD, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Mar Ivanios College, Nalanchira, Thiruvananthapuram, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Zoology, Mar Ivanios College, Nalanchira, Thiruvananthapuram, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Globally, the environmental pollution is one of the major issues causing toxicity towards human and aquatic life. We have developed a facile and innovative sensing approach for detection of sulphide ions (S2−) present in the aqueous media using Ag0 decorated Cr2S3 NPs embedded on PVP matrix (Ag/Cr2S3-PVP). Based on the SPR phenomena, the detection of S2− ions was established. The nanohybrid was characterized using various techniques such as UV–vis spectrophotometer, High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Thermal Gravimetric Analysis (TGA), X-ray diffraction analysis(XRD), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The yellowish colour of Ag/Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid turned to brown colour in presence of S2− ions. The selectivity and sensitivity of the prepared probe was studied against the other interfering metal ions. In addition, the effect of different concentration of S2− ions in the nanohybrid solution was investigated and the Limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 6.6 nM. The good linearity was found over the range of 10 nM to 100 μM with R2 value of 0.981. The paper strip based probe was developed for rapid onsite monitoring of S2− ions. The proposed method is found to be cost-effective, rapid, and simple. We have validated the practical applicability of the prepared probe for determining the concentration of S2− ions in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulphides are largely exists in the by-products of industrial production, medical synthesis, and military industry [1]. Sulphide is one of the poisonous anion used in various industries widely such as gas making, paper making, dyeing, cooking, mineral dressing and leather making etc [2]. It has also been generated during the organic matter decomposition [3]. High sulphide compounds concentrations in air and water can lead to severe toxic effects in the environment as well as for human which pose great risks. The high amount of sulphide smoking can cause serious diseases such as liver cirrhosis, high blood pressure, diabetes, and down syndrome [4]. The sulfur content is used as a common indices in china to check the quality of drinking water. According to the regulations, the sulphide concentrations should not exceed 1 mg/L in ground water and 0.02 mg/L in drinking water. In human, sulphide reacts with cytochrome and oxidase where it affects the oxidation processes occurring in biological cells causing hypoxia state in biological somatic tissue and eventually threatens the biological life [5,6]. At acidic state, sulphide ion converts to SO2 as it is unstable and in this form it is said to be highly toxic causing serious risks to the environment such as metal objects corrosion, lake acidification, weathering, historical relics, forest area reduction and so on. Thus, sulphide ion is the most toxic pollutant in the environment existing widely in the industrial waste water and natural water. Hence, it is an important index to be monitored in water. The rapid quantitative and qualitative S2− ion concentration determination are crucial for monitoring the water bodies and preventing the S2−pollution.

Various methods such as fluorometric method [7], chromatography [8], capillary electrophoresis [9] and electrochemical method [10] are adopted for sensing of sulphide (S2−) ions. These techniques require technical operators and expensive instruments. The tedious operation and the pre-processing consumes lot of time and instant information cannot be provided which limits its practical applicability for rapid sulphide ion detection. These methods have certain limitations such as poor sensitivity, time consumption, requires sophisticated skills and large sample volumes. Colorimetric methods are attracted by many researchers because of its low cost and simplicity.

Chromium sulphide (Cr2S3) is a semiconductor material with good electrical and magnetic properties [11]. The properties of Cr2S3 have been studied and reported by several researchers [11-13]. The photocatalytic activity of Cr2S3 was investigated by Hussain et al. [14] using single source and multi source precursors. The magnetic, optical, and electrical properties of the transition metal chalcogenides can be modified through incorporation of suitable dopants such as silver [15-21]. The metal nanoparticles combined with semiconductor sulphides have been used in sensing and colorimetric detection of analyte in the environmental system [22-24]. Ag NPs have been used as a dopant where it possesses surface plasmon resonance (SPR) property. Ag NPs are used for the colorimetric detection of various analyte [25-27]. SPR is where the photon energy is absorbed by free electron on the metal surface during light irradiation and it is called as the collective oscillation phenomenon [28-31]. The unique optical absorption, sensing applications and photothermal conversion property is provided by this phenomenon to the metal NPs especially to silver and gold [32,33]. The previous studies have proven that the characteristics of SPR exhibited by the noble metal NPs can be related very closely to the size, shape and structure [34,35].

The total dissolved sulphide in the aqueous solution are present in two forms: unionized form that is H2S and as ionized forms that is HS−, and S2− [36]. The PVP was used a matrix to entrap the Cr2S3 doped Ag. On adding S2− ion, Ag gets oxidized to Ag2S. On introducing S2− ion, the Ag2S NPs are formed on the Cr2S3 surface which is embedded on the surface of PVP matrix which can cause increase in absorption spectra of the NCs and a prominent visual colour change. This unique feature makes it compatible with the rapid response of spectra on adding S2− into the PVP-Ag/Cr2S3 NPs solution.

Herein, we have reported an easy to use, rapid colorimetric S2− ions detection using Ag/Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid. The S2− ions detected using Ag/Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid where highly sensitive and selective. Also, the method adopted is very simple without the requirement of complex instrumentation.
Chromium nitrate, sodium sulphide, silver nitrate, PVP, and the metal ions were procured from Sisco research laboratories Pvt Ltd, India. All chemicals procured were of analytical grade.
The characterization results are provided in the supplementary material Text S1.

The study used chromium nitrate as a precursor for preparing Cr2S3 NPs. The chromium nitrate solution of 0.1 M was prepared by dissolving 1.19 g in 50 mL distilled water (Solution A). The 0.1 M Na2S solution was synthesized on dissolving 0.639 g in 50 mL distilled water (Solution B). In solution B, solution A was added drop by drop. The stirring was continued for 2 h at 80 °C. The obtained Cr2S3 was centrifuged and washed several times with ethanol and dis·H2O. The Cr2S3 NPs was dried at 80 °C for 2 h.

Then, 25 mg of prepared Cr2S3 NPs was dispersed in 50 mL dis·H2O. The ice-chilled NaBH4 of 1 mM was prepared (30 mL) and 20 mL of 1% PVP was mixed into the ice chilled NaBH4. The 3 mL of the dispersed Cr2S3 NPs were added to the mixture containing PVP and NaBH4. Afterwards, AgNO3 solution was added drop wise into the mixture. The yellow colour indicates the successful formation of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid.

The synthesized probe was used for the S2− ions detection without further modification. The S2− ion of 1 mM was added into the Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid in 3:2 ratio. The selectivity of the probe towards S2− was investigated by adding various metal ions such as chromium, cadmium, potassium, zinc, nickel, sodium, manganese, fluorine, and sulphide (1 mM) to Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid separately. Various S2− ions concentrations (1 nM to 1 mM) were added to the nanohybrid for determining the range of S2− ions detection by the prepared probe. The interference of other metal ions in S2− ions detection was also investigated. The various metal ions of 1 mM were mixed with S2− and it was used to study the interference in S2− detection. The experiment was also performed for controls only in metal ions presence and S2− ion absence. Six replicates were performed to prevent the experimental errors.

The prepared probe was optimized at different environmental conditions such as pH, temperature and saline to understand the parameters favoring the detection. The S2− of 1 μM concentration was used for the study. The study was performed from pH 5.0 to 9.0, the temperature range from 20 °C to 70 °C and the salt concentrations from 0.1 to 0.8%.

The portable S2− detection probe was developed for onsite monitoring of S2−. The Whatmann No. 1 filter paper was cut into pieces (1 × 1 cm) and soaked in the Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nano hybrid solution for 2 h. Then, the soaked strips were dried at room temperature for 1 h. The paper strips impregnated with nanohybrid were tested by spiking different metal ions for the quantitative detection of S2−. Also, different S2− concentrations were spiked to the paper strip probe to evaluate its sensitivity in detection.

The pond water was collected from Sathyamangalam, Erode, Tamil Nadu and tap water was collected from Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Erode, Tamil Nadu. The river water was collected from Bhavani river, Erode, India. The Whatman No: 1 filter paper was used to separate the impurities present in tap water. The proposed probe was implemented in real water sample which is collected from STP of Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Erode, Tamil Nadu. The known S2− concentration was spiked into the samples and the efficiency of the prepared probe to detect S2− was determined

The yellow colored Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid showed λmax at 404 nm due to its SPR property determined using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The S2− ions interaction caused the colour variation from yellow colour of the probe to colorless. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the prepared nanohybrid is shown in Fig. 1 a.

The XRD data was recorded to determine the structural properties of the synthesized Cr2S3, and Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid by using X-ray diffractometer. Fig. 1b depicts the XRD pattern of Cr2S3, and Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid. The diffraction peaks of Cr2S3 appeared at 2θ of 27.85°, 34.33°, 36.11°, 39.54°, 47.90° and 51.33° assigned to (104), (113), (210), (240), (180), and (330) reflections respectively [37]. The crystal system of the synthesized Cr2S3 is found to be rhombohedral. The volume of the unit cell is 508.59. The lattice parameters for the rhombohedral Cr2S3 are a = 5.93 Å; b = 5.93 Å; c = 16.65 Å, α = 90°; β = 90°; γ = 120°. The calculated density and volume of the cell for Cr2S3 is 3.92 g/cm3 and 508.59 pm3. The poly crystalline phase of Cr2S3 corresponds to rhombohedral crystal system with space group of R-3 verified using XRD results.

The Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid possessed diffractions peaks at 2θ of 27.85°, 34.33°, 36.11°, 39.54°, 47.90° and 51.33° assigned to (104), (113), (210), (240), (180), and (330) reflections for Cr2S3. The characteristics reflections of Ag peaks at 2θ of 38.64°, 44.23°, 64.4° and 77.48° were observed and matches with the JCPDS no. 65-2871. These peaks confirm the decoration of Ag on Cr2S3. To calculate the crystalline size of the nanohybrid, scherrer’s formula was used as follows:
(1)
S =
k λ
β c o s θ
where S is the crystallite size, k is a constant which is assumed to be 0.9, λ is the wavelength of the X-ray radiation, β is the line width and θ is the angle of diffraction (Eq. (1)). The average crystalline size of the synthesized Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid obtained from scherrer formula in XRD data was found to be 23.09 nm. The zeta potential and particle size of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid was determined and it was found to be 24 ± 1.9 nm and −33 ± 1.3 mV respectively. The particles were found to be polydispersed and the nanohybrid exhibited the zeta potential of −33 ± 1.5 mV even after one month of incubation which indicates its stability.

The FT-IR spectrum of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid and Cr2S3 are shown in Fig. 1c. In FTIR spectra of Cr2S3 NPs, the peak at 533 and 630 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of CrS bonds. In FTIR spectra of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid, the peaks at 1115, 1318, 1411 and 1650 cm−1 attributes to CH, CH2, CN, and CO stretching vibrations of PVP respectively which confirms that Ag@Cr2S3 was embedded on PVP. The peak at 3350, 3286 and 3257 cm−1 belongs to OH stretching vibration. The peaks indicated the formation of nanohybrid.

HR-TEM is found to be an efficient instrument to magnify the materials images and it can be used to visualize nanomaterials [34]. The morphology and structure of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP was investigated using HR-TEM and it is depicted in Fig. 2 a. The particles formed were spherical in shape where PVP acts as a matrix. Here, the probe Ag@Cr2S3 embedded on PVP matrix. The heterjunction is confirmed from Fig. 2b. The inset of Fig. 2b represents the SAED pattern of nanohybrid. The particles formed were of varying sizes. The lattice fringes of 0.24 and 0.28 nm are assigned to (111) planes of Ag and (202) planes of Cr2S3 (Fig. 2c) [38]. The heterojunction formation is confirmed by the two different lattice fringes. The results suggest that the deposition of Ag on Cr2S3 were successful. The chemical composition of the nanohybrid was investigated using EDAX. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrum of the prepared Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid is shown in Supplementary material Fig. S1a. The EDAX confirmed the presence of O, S, Ag, and Cr with weight percentage of 17.17, 7.7, 0.74 and 61.37% respectively.

The TGA curves of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid shown in Supplementary material Fig. S1c. At rate of 10 °C/min, the known amount of sample was heated under nitrogen flow up to 700 °C, where this temperature is in between the solvent’s boiling point and polymer degradation temperature. The three stages of weight losses are observed for PVP (Supplementary material Fig. S1c). Initially, the weight loss was 6.5% up to 250 °C temperature. The loss in weight up to 250 °C attributes to the loss of moisture and break down of low molecular weight oligomers, and residual solvents. At second stage, the weight loss was 12% in the temperature range from 250 to 396 °C. This weight loss was due to the structural decomposition of polymer. At third stage, 39% weight loss occurred in the temperature range from 396 to 695 °C. The total weight loss was about 39%. This shows that the polymer is stable when it is in nanohybrid form.

The specific surface area and porosity was calculated using nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm of the nanohybrid. The specific surface area of the nanohybrid is 44.1 m2/g. The pore diameter and pore volume was calculated using BETmethod. The pore diameter and pore volume of the prepared probe is 1.57 nm and 0.116 cm3/g respectively.

The XPS was used to understand the chemical states and composition of prepared probe. Fig. 3 b shows the survey spectrum of nanohybrid. The presence of elements was confirmed with the XPS spectrum. The elements such as Cr, O, S and Ag are present before interaction with S2−. Fig. 3b1 shows the XPS spectra of S 2p region. The S 2p contains two peaks at 168.82 and 162.9 eV corresponds to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 [38] (Fig. 3b). The two fitting curves of O 1s ascribed to OH of water molecule and hydroxyl moiety. The O1s peaks at 530.99 eV correspond to O2− ions in the bonding structure of CrO (Fig. 3c). Fig. 2d shows the XPS spectra of Cr 2p where the two peaks at 586.85 and 576.91 eV attributed to Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2. The two peaks of Ag 3d shown in Fig. 3e at 372.46 and 367.41 eV corresponds to Ag 3d1/2 and Ag 3d3/2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the analytical process
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a shows the Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid image after interaction with metal ions. Here, colour change from yellow to brown was observed only with S2− solution and there were no observable colour change noted with all other metal ions. The similar trend was observed in the UV–visible spectrophotometric study. Fig. 4b shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid after interaction with different metal ions. The peak intensity increased and there is a slight blue shift in the peak was observed only with S2−. The study shows that the probe was selective towards S2− ion. UV–visible absorption spectra of nanohybrid at various S2− concentrations from 1 nM to 1 mM are represented in Fig. 4c. The peak intensity of nanohybrid decreased with increase of S2− concentration. The linear correlation exists (y = 0.061x + 0.807, R2 = 0.981) in the plot between the different concentration of S2− and the absorbance at 404 nm (Fig. 4d), which suggests the possible practical application of the prepared probe.

The limit of detection of the prepared probe was calculated using the following equation:
LOD =
3 σ
slope
o f
t h e
c a l i b r a t i o n
c u r v e
where σ is the standard deviation. The limit of detection of the prepared probe calculated using the formula and it is determined to be 6.6 nM. The limit of detection obtained in the present study is found to be better than the previously reported probes for S2− detection (Table 1 ). On comparing with the methods, the colorimetric method developed newly is found to be cost-effective and it suits very well for routine S2−monitoring.

Fig. 4d shows the interference study where the absence of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid showed only a negligible change with other metal ions interacted (in the absence of S2− ions). The peak intensity increased in S2− presence with other metal ions which was almost similar to the detection of individual S2− (Fig. 4c).

The effect of pH, NaCl and temperature on S2− detection was investigated to optimize the environmental parameters for the detection. The detection was effective at pH 7 (Fig. 5 a) and the detection was found to be decreased with increase of pH value. Fig. 5b represents the effect of temperature on S2− detection and the result show that the temperature did not influence the effective detection. Fig. 5c shows the effect of NaCl concentration and least effect were observed in the detection of S2− ions in NaCl presence.

The synthesized probe was used successfully for the detection of spiked S2− ions from tap water, pond water, drinking water and river. The S2− was not detected in the collected samples before the spiking of S2− ions. To further confirm the feasibility of the proposed method in terms of its application to real samples for determination, the recovery experiments were performed using samples spiked with 0.5 μM of S2− ions. The spiked samples were exposed to the prepared probe and the detected concentrations and its efficiency in recovery is represented in Table 2 . The unknown sulphide solution was test using the prepared probe. The results revealed that low level of sulphide is present in the sample. The results indicated that the prepared probe is reliable and it can be practically applied for the estimation of S2− ions from real samples.

The Whatmann No. 1 filter paper was cut into small pieces and soaked in Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid solution for 2 h. Then, the soaked strips were dried at room temperature for 1 h. The colour of the strip changed to yellow which indicates the successful impregnation of nanohybrid on the strip. The prepared paper strip was tested by spiking different ions (Fig. 6 a). It was observed that the strip spiked with S2− ions alone have showed colour change. Fig. 6b shows the prepared paper strip interacted with different ions. The different concentration of S2− ions was spiked on the paper strip from 1 mM to 1 nM where the brown colour decreased with increase of S2− ions concentrations. The prepared paper strip was found to be highly sensitive and selective for real time detection of S2− ions.

Based on the results observed from XPS, FT-IR spectra, EDAX, particle size distribution, we hypothesized a possible mechanism for the detection of S2− ions using Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid (Fig. 7 ). The interaction of S2− ions with Ag0 of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP caused the formation of Ag2S (Ksp ≈ 10−51, in water) via the mechanism of direct particle-fluid reaction [47]:
(1) S2− + H2O ⇌ SH− + OH−
(2) 4Ag + 2SH− + O2 ⟶ 2Ag2S + 2OH−
(3) 4Ag + 2S2− + O2 + 2H2O ⟶ 2Ag2S + 4OH−

The conversion of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP to Ag2S@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid (Ag2S from Ag) [47,51] observed due to the interaction of S2− ions with Ag0 which is decorated on the surface of Cr2S3 to form Ag2S. The sulfidation of Ag in Ag@Cr2S3 have lead to the increase in peak intensity and hence the detection of S2− ions possible. XPS study confirmed the Ag, Cr, and S presence. The fitting curve and deconvulation of Ag 3d and S 2p spectra is shown in Fig. 3e and b, respectively. The binding energy of Ag 3d5/2 (from 367.41 eV to 368.64 eV) and Ag 3d3/2 (from 372.4 to 374.45 eV) showed decreased peaks for Ag@Cr2S3 after S2− treatment (Fig. 3d) due to the presence of both Ag (0) and Ag (I) in the treated sample [52,53]. The Fitting curve and deconvulation of S 2p peak for S2− treated Ag@Cr2S3 have resulted in peaks shift at 167.85 and 163.3 eV to 168.82 and 162.9 eV (Fig. 3b), attributes to the S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 of S2− in Ag2S, formed after interaction with S2− ions [54]. In Fig. 3d, there is a slight shift in the binding energy of Cr 2p from 587.87 to 586.85 eV due to the increase in the electron density. The band width of Cr has increased from 1.4 to 1.6 due to the formation of Ag2S on Cr2S3. The size of the particle has increased from 21 nm to 225 nm due to the formation of Ag2S. The particle size has increased when S2− ions interacted specifically with Ag decorated Cr2S3 embedded on PVP matrix to form Ag2S [54]. The increase in intensity of the SPR peak of nanohybrid is related to the particle size increase [55,56]. The prepared probe exhibited good colorimetric recognition and selectiveness towards S2− ions.

The zeta potential and particle size for nanohybrid after interaction with different S2− ion at different concentrations were determined. The particle size and zeta potential of nanohybrid was 31 ± 2 nm and −29.12 ± 2.5 mV respectively when it was interacted with 1 nM S2− ions. The particle size of nanohybrid was increased to 131 ± 6 nm and the zeta potential was decreased to and −21 ± 0.7 mV when the nanohybrid was exposed to 1 mM S2− ions.

In the FT-IR spectra, the formation of Ag2S is confirmed with the appearance of peak at 605 cm−1 corresponds to the AgS bond. In FTIR spectra of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid, the peaks at 1115, 1318, 1411 and 1650 cm−1 attributes to CH, CH2, CN, and CO stretching vibrations of PVP respectively which confirms that Ag@Cr2S3 embedded on PVP. The peak at 3350, 3286, 3257 cm−1 belongs to OH stretching vibration. The Ag-S peak appeared due to the specific interaction of S2− ions with Ag0 present on the surface of Cr2S3. The interaction of S2− ions with Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid is evident from the FT-IR spectra of the probe after the detection study which confirms the detection ability of probe towards S2− ions. The SEM image of NCs before interaction with S2− ions is depicted in Supplementary material Fig. S1d. The morphology of the NCs is changed after interaction with S2− ions (Supplementary material Fig. S1e). The image clearly indicates that the particle size increased after interaction with S2− ions. Thus, the particle size distribution, FT-IR and XPS data supports the possible mechanism of detection of S2− ions. The EDAX spectra after interaction with S2− ions, the weight % of sulphur have increased from 7.78 to 9.95% (Fig. S1b). This indicates that S2− ions have interacted with the nanohybrid.

The Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid was synthesized by chemical co-precipitation and chemical reduction method. The nanohybrid was characterized by various techniques such as UV–Vis, FT-IR, HR-TEM, XPS, and XRD techniques. The SPR band absorbance change was the basis for the colour change where the LOD of the probe was found to be 6.6 nM. The interaction with S2− ions has caused the aggregation of Ag@Cr2S3-PVP nanohybrid which was highly selective and it can be determined without the aid of complicated instruments and preparation of samples. The probe have well performed in presence of interference ions. The on-site S2− ions monitoring was done using the prepared paper strip based probe. The new method developed has significant advantage over the methods reported previously includes wide range detection, simple instrument and convenience in operation. This system has excellent properties which makes its practical applications to detect S2− ions from various aqueous samples. The strategy adopted for sensing can be applied in potential applications related to life sciences and biotechnology.

S. Kokilavani: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Asad Syed: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Ajith M. Thomas: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Abdallah M. Elgorban: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Ali H. Bahkali: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Nouf S.S. Zaghloul: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Lija L. Raju: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. S. Sudheer Khan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors sincerely thank the management of Bannari Amman Institute of Technology, Tamil Nadu for providing the necessary facilities for carrying out this work. The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP-2021/56), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120253.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
